name: Starting a new D&D campaign
summary:
**Conversation Overview**

This was an extended session zero for a tabletop RPG system called "The Deep Salts," a custom game Jake is designing and playtesting simultaneously. Jake runs as both player and designer, and Claude served as DM while also managing rules arbitration, gap identification, and document maintenance throughout. The session had two parallel tracks: actual play (running Prettyboy Lloyd through his first run) and live game design work (flagging inconsistencies, proposing rulings, and updating canonical documents).

The session began with character creation for "Prettyboy Lloyd," a young athletic fighter wielding self-made dual knuckledusters rigged with detonating powder capsules. Character stats were negotiated through backstory (Quick/Skill-governed weapon, base 22 damage, Powder Charge gimmick), with a final attribute spread of VIG 8 / END 10 / STR 4 / SKI 17 / RES 3 at Level 2 by session's end. Lloyd completed a full run through an unnamed first biome: killing three Waterlogged Guests and one Chapel Penitent, discovering a Wrap item and the "Choir Deep" recipe (unbrewed), banking 39 White Salts, and reaching Insight 4. Key narrative threads established include an old man stuck behind an Insight-gated threshold (Lloyd attempted to carry him through; failed per the ruling), and the reception clerk repeatedly referencing Lloyd's "brother" in an unresolved, deliberate hook.

Several rulings were made during play and canonized into the documents: visceral charges removed as a resource entirely (parries now gated by timing and a genuine telegraphed opening only); enemy limb stagger grants an immediate free called shot on the triggering hit, with variable stagger duration set per Monster Template; small high-value called-shot targets (bells, eyes, cracks) are mechanically limbs using the Sever system; Influence is uncapped past 10 with DM-discretion severity scaling rather than a fixed overflow trigger; self-initiated called shots without an enemy tell use a tighter tolerance band and end the turn on a miss; Maud's salt-ID pricing confirmed at 5 White Salts doubling per ID. Two Endurance perks (Overcharge/Endless) were orphaned by charge removal and marked provisional. Currency terminology was standardized to "White Salts" throughout mechanical references, preserving "The Token That Isn't Yours" and other flavor-text uses as intentional. Jake's stated preferences throughout: concrete rulings with specific numbers over menus or options, brief responses, flagging gaps explicitly rather than patching them silently, and player rolls their own dice. Corrections noted: Claude spoiled enemy mechanics mid-combat (told not to), rolled player dice without permission (told to stop), and generated a stopwatch widget with no visual anchor making it impossible to use fairly.

At session's end, three updated project documents were produced (ruleset v7.1, design bible v2.6, dev log v1.4) plus a new campaign log for narrative continuity. All five files were pushed to the GitHub repo at `pixel549/the-deep-salts` using a token stored in project info. Claude also recommended adding an instruction to project info directing future instances to pull the campaign log and character sheet at session start and push updates at session end.

**Tool Knowledge**

GitHub operations were performed using bash with HTTPS authentication via a fine-grained personal access token stored in project info. The working pattern for cloning and pushing is: `git clone "https://${GH_TOKEN}@github.com/[owner]/[repo].git"` and `git push "https://${GH_TOKEN}@github.com/[owner]/[repo].git"` — passing the token inline in the URL rather than through a credential helper, since the sandbox has no persistent credential store. Token values were masked in logged output using `sed "s/${GH_TOKEN}/[TOKEN]/g"` piped after git commands. File edits were done by copying project files to `/home/claude/docs/` as writable working copies (source files at `/mnt/project/` are read-only), editing there, then committing. The repo is at `pixel549/the-deep-salts`. Key files: `deep-salts-v7.1.md` (ruleset), `deep-salts-design-bible-v2.6.md`, `deep-salts-dev-log-v1.4.md`, `deep-salts-character-

content.text: dang it. I just lost my character sheet. lets start a new one, call this session zero for a new campaign

content.thinking:
Jake lost his character sheet and wants to start a fresh campaign of The Deep Salts with a proper session zero. I should run the backstory-negotiated character creation process from the ruleset rather than just discussing rules—this is actual play facilitation, not design work. He prefers short, succinct responses and wants me to defer to his judgment when needed.

I'll keep things conversational and collaborative since session zero is about working together on character concept, not narrating in-fiction yet. I should ask about his character concept and backstory to kick things off, then work through attributes, starting weapon, and gimmick based on what he wants to play. I'll hold off on creating the character sheet file until we've worked through the negotiation together. No need to dig through past chats either—he's starting fresh. Keep it casual and brief.

summaries.summary: Thinking about creating a new tabletop RPG character sheet for campaign session zero.
Facilitating character creation through backstory negotiation.

content.text:
Ouch. Alright — clean slate, new campaign, session zero it is.

Per the ruleset (§4), character creation is backstory-negotiated, not menu-picked: you write a short concept, I push back on anything that breaks the Damage Floor or gives something for nothing, we land on numbers together.

So — who's checking into Stillwell Hydro? Give me the concept: who they are, why they're here, and roughly what they're bringing into a fight (or not bringing — a coward with a knife is as valid as a former soldier). Doesn't need to be long.

A young, handsome man, blonde hair, bright blue eyes - but there is weariness, darkness in them. Athletic build, potentially some kind of fighter. In his pocket, he carries two weapons of his own creation - both are a slab of metal, slightly ridged on one side, with holes for his fingers on the other side. Both of his "Metal Knuckles" as he calls them, also feature a small space on the side to insert a capsule of explosive powder (or anything else that might heighten his strikes), and a pressure release that will detonate that powder outwards when a heavy enough blow is struck.

content.thinking:
I'm working through the mechanical design for a fighter character with dual metal knuckles that have an explosive-capsule gimmick, translating the concept into attribute allocation from a 40-point pool, a custom starting weapon with appropriate damage scaling, and a signature gimmick that balances cost against benefit.

For the weapon itself, dual knuckles fit the Quick type governed by Skill since they're finesse-based striking tools rather than raw power weapons. Base damage should sit slightly above the floor of 20—maybe 22-24 with a D-grade rating—reflecting that this is a well-designed homemade weapon from an athletic fighter, not something dramatically overpowered for a starting character.

The real hook is the gimmick: explosive powder capsules that detonate on heavy strikes. This works as a consumable resource mechanic—you load a capsule before combat (or spend an action to load mid-fight), and when you land a Heavy attack, it detonates for bonus damage. The cost is both the limited supply of capsules you need to acquire and carry, plus some inherent risk or unreliability to keep it balanced against the reward.

For the weapon itself, I'm thinking Quick-type, one-handed knuckles that you wield as a pair, similar to how the Tideglass Cleaver notes the "Optional" two-handed property—dual metal knuckles function as a matched set but don't need to be treated as a special dual-wield system.

Since I need to stick to one core gimmick, I'm focusing on the explosive capsule detonation: inserting a capsule costs a Fast Action mid-combat, then triggering it requires a stopwatch check like a called shot. Success means bonus explosive damage plus maybe an Off-Balance effect on the target; failure causes backfire damage to yourself.

For scaling, the bonus should feel meaningful but not overshadow existing weapons—something like +50% of the heavy attack's damage or a flat bonus tied to capsule quality. The status effect from the concussive blast fits thematically. I also need to limit capsule availability to 3-5 per carry, treating them as consumable resources similar to other item limits.

Now I'm thinking about which attribute governs this weapon—the athletic, technical fighter vibe suggests Skill might be the better fit for quick knuckle-based combat, though I should check what feels right for the build.

For the attribute spread, I'm considering a redistribution from the default flat 8 across all five stats. Given the athletic fighter concept, something like Skill 14, Endurance 10, and Vigor 8 could work, though I'll let the player adjust based on whether the weariness and darkness in their eyes suggests grit or something else entirely. bers. Since Jake prefers concrete proposals over menus, I'll put together a full character build: "The Fighter"—a young, athletic guy with homemade metal knuckles rigged with explosive capsules. I'm allocating the 40 attribute points as Skill 16 (his weapon demands precision), Endurance 10 (mobile and athletic), Vigor 8 (baseline), Strength 4 (he's speed-focused, not strength-based), and Resolve 2 (deliberately low—reflecting the trauma and weariness that's worn him down, making him vulnerable to mental strain). Now I'm calculating the derived stats from these attributes. Low Resolve doesn't immediately hurt the save bonus since both 2 and 8 floor to 0, but it does create a roleplay vulnerability and makes future leveling more costly to reach the next tier—a fair mechanical trade-off. Let me verify that raw Endurance scores only matter for linear formulas like HP and Movement, not for ESV calculations.

Now I'm deciding on the Metal Knuckles setup—they're paired one-handed weapons used together, so I need to clarify whether they should be classified as Two-Handed or kept as a single Quick weapon system mechanically.

For damage scaling, I'm calculating the base of 22 (slightly above the floor to reflect basic fighter competence) with Light and Heavy variants using the ESV multipliers. With Skill 16 mapping to ESV 16 in the 1-20 band, Light damage comes to 30 and Heavy to 56, but I need to confirm the ESV value at the character's starting point. So at Skill 40 the ESV jumps to 33, pushing Light damage to 39 and Heavy to 69. By Skill 99 with an ESV around 47, Light settles near 46 and Heavy around 79. For the passive ability, I'm designing "Powder Charge"—a mechanic where you load an explosive capsule into your knuckles as a quick action, then trigger it on a successful Heavy attack with a risky detonation check that adds 50% of the heavy's damage as bonus explosive damage and applies a disorienting effect to the target.

If the timing check fails, the capsule backfires and you take a Blood Loss stack from shrapnel instead, losing the consumable either way. Capsules are limited (carry 4, restocked at the hub or found in the world), and you can load both knuckles independently but only detonate one per Heavy landed. This keeps the risk-reward tight and uses the existing stopwatch check system without introducing new mechanics.

The weapon doesn't gate behind Insight — it's available from Insight 0 like the Paring Knife. Checking sever viability: the damage caps out around 79-90 on Heavy, well below sever thresholds (120 for head, 308 for arm, 400 for leg), so this stays in Skill/Quick weapon territory rather than becoming a sever tool. That's the right call and consistent with how weapons should be honest about what they can and can't do.

For the Two-Handed field, since this is a paired set of one-handers rather than a single weapon requiring both hands, I'll mark it as N/A and note that it's dual-wielded.

Now I need to pull together the full character sheet proposal—attributes, weapons, the core gimmick—and present it concisely with concrete numbers, the way Jake prefers. Since this is session zero, I should start with the backstory negotiation, but given the instruction to propose rulings directly rather than offering menus, I'll present the complete proposal upfront and ask for confirmation or adjustments. I'll keep it tight: the attribute table, weapon block, the gimmick explanation, then invite feedback before moving into the opening scene. I also need to set starting Insight at 1 per the ruleset and confirm the character level.

For level, the baseline 8-flat-in-all is the starting point, and redistributing those same 40 points through backstory negotiation doesn't count as spent White Salts—that's just the concept translation. So the character starts at Level 0 (no progression points spent yet), matching the precedent from the worked example. I should also ask for the character's name to round out the sheet. I should hold off on creating the actual character sheet file until we've negotiated and confirmed the numbers—character creation is a back-and-forth process where the DM pushes back and the player adjusts, so I'll present the proposal now, invite feedback, and only generate the file once we've landed on final stats.

summaries.summary: Calibrating fighter attributes and balancing explosive gimmick mechanics.
Deliberating whether Skill governs the explosive knuckles weapon.
Calibrating damage scaling and attribute allocation for fighter build.
Synthesizing complete character sheet with concrete mechanics and attributes.
Architected fighter build with explosive knuckle mechanics and attribute allocation.

content.text:
Alright. Let's build this guy.

**Reading the concept:** dual self-made knuckles, capsule-rigged for detonation on a heavy blow — that's a Quick weapon, Skill-governed (fast, technical striker, not a strength brute), with one gimmick: the powder charge. Here's my translation. Push back on anything.

**Attributes** (40-point pool, redistributed from flat 8s):

| VIG | END | STR | SKI | RES |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 10 | 4 | 16 | 2 |

Skill carries the build — he's fast and precise, not strong. Resolve dumped hard: the weariness in his eyes isn't free narratively *or* mechanically — he's fraying, worse Insanity/Influence saves as those tracks build. That's the trade for a 16 Skill start. Tell me if that's not the read you want.

**Weapon — "Metal Knuckles" (paired)**
- Type: Quick · Skill / D · One-handed (paired, no two-hand bonus — already priced in)
- Base Damage: 22 (floor is 20 — the craftsmanship earns him 2, nothing more)
- Light = 22 + ESV×0.5 · Heavy = 44 + ESV×0.75
- At Skill 16 (start): Light 30 · Heavy 56

**Gimmick — Powder Charge**
Load a capsule into a knuckle (Fast Action). Land a **Heavy** with one loaded: stopwatch detonation check —
- **Hit the window:** +50% of that heavy's raw damage, target takes 1 Discombobulation tick.
- **Miss it:** capsule vents wrong — he eats 1 self-inflicted Blood Loss stack. Capsule's spent either way.

Carry limit 4 capsules, restocked at the hub. This is a status/burst tool, not a sever weapon — even at max Skill this thing doesn't get near a 120-raw head sever. If you wanted him to eventually decapitate things with his fists, that's a different weapon down the line, not this one.

Two things I need from you: **his name**, and does the Resolve dump sit right, or do you want to shift a point or two out of Skill to cover it?
so skill is qwhat will influence the damage from the knuckles? Not Strength?

content.thinking:
Looking at the ruleset to understand how weapon damage scales—Strength governs heavy weapons and limb-severing power, while Skill handles light weapon damage and precision mechanics. The weapon table confirms this with examples like the Tideglass Cleaver being Skill-based and the Cautery Saw being Strength-based.

The key insight is that each weapon has a single governing stat tied to its type (Quick, Heavy, or Reach), and that stat applies to both its light and heavy attacks. So Metal Knuckles, being a Quick-type weapon, scales off Skill for all its attacks—not Strength. The user's question about why Skill instead of Strength makes sense given the weapon's classification.

I should acknowledge this is a reasonable point to negotiate during character creation. I can explain the reasoning (Quick weapons are Skill-governed by the system's pattern), but if they want a Strength-governed knuckles weapon, that would shift it to a different weapon type entirely—trading the fast dual-wield style for heavier, slower hits. I'll keep my answer direct and let them push back if they want to explore that option.

summaries.summary: Thinking about damage calculation mechanics comparing skill versus strength attributes.
Reconciling weapon scaling mechanics between Strength and Skill attributes.
Reconciled weapon classification system with damage scaling mechanics.

content.text:
Type, not the fictional weapon. Per §4:

- **STRENGTH** → Heavy-type weapons, limb-sever power
- **SKILL** → Quick-type weapons, parry/precision tolerance

Look at the table: Cautery Saw (Heavy) is Strength/B. Tideglass Cleaver and Birthing Hook (Quick/Reach) are both Skill. The governing stat is locked to weapon *type*, not to "does this look like a strength thing."

I called the Knuckles Quick because the concept reads as fast, technical, twin-wielded — closer to the Cleaver than the Saw. That's a build choice though, not a fixed fact about knuckledusters. If you want him to hit like a truck instead of like a boxer, we make them Heavy/Strength instead — but then:

- Slower, one big swing at a time instead of a fast flurry
- Governed by Strength, so that 16 Skill / 4 Strength split flips — he'd need Strength up and Skill can drop
- Actually opens a real sever path down the line, which Quick/Skill knuckles never will

Which version is he? Fast brawler or heavy hitter?
he could open a sever pathway later with new Skill weapons though, right?

content.thinking:
I'm checking the ruleset for how Strength affects limb-severing mechanics. According to the rules, Strength directly determines heavy weapon damage and sever power, and I can calculate viability thresholds for each limb by dividing the sever requirement by the weapon's multiplier—heads need 120 raw minimum, arms around 308, and legs 400. The Paring Knife falls short of these thresholds across all limbs regardless of stat scaling.

Now I'm reconsidering whether the sever mechanic is truly restricted to Strength weapons only. Quick weapons can also have heavy attacks using the same formula, so theoretically a Skill-governed weapon could deal enough raw damage to sever if it reaches the threshold. Testing the Tideglass Cleaver as a Skill/C weapon at ESV 47, the heavy attack formula yields roughly 99 raw damage—still below the 120 needed for head sever. The ruleset description ties sever power specifically to Strength, but I'm questioning whether that's a mechanical restriction or just flavor text. Let me actually work through the math: a Quick weapon with base 20, maxed Skill (ESV around 47), and S-grade would hit roughly 131 raw on a heavy hit—that's above the 120 head sever threshold. So technically a late-game Skill weapon could sever, though it'd need top-tier gear to get there, whereas Heavy weapons reach that threshold much more naturally by design. 'd have higher base damage. So the math checks out: a Skill weapon can technically reach sever thresholds, but it requires finding a higher-grade option with better base stats than the current Knuckles, whereas Strength weapons naturally tend to have those advantages built in.

I should give him a direct answer with the numbers rather than hedging—his current Knuckles won't sever no matter how high Skill goes, so if he wants that pathway, he needs a different weapon, not just more levels.

summaries.summary: Thinking about game mechanics enabling future skill weapon progression pathways.
Calculating sever thresholds across limbs and weapons.
Examined damage thresholds and determined higher-tier Skill weapons could theoretically sever.

content.text:
Yes, mechanically — sever isn't stat-locked, it's raw-damage-locked. The formula (§10) is just min raw = threshold ÷ multiplier. It doesn't check which attribute got you there.

Strength/Heavy weapons are just the *practical* path because they start with bigger bases and access B/A/S grades more readily (Cautery Saw: base 30, grade B). But do the math on a Skill/Quick weapon at grade S:

Heavy = 2×Base + ESV×grade×1.5. Base 22, grade S (×1.3), ESV 47 (maxed Skill):
2×22 + 47×1.3×1.5 = 44 + 91.65 = **135.65 raw** — clears the head's 120 threshold with room to spare.

So a *late-game, high-grade* Skill weapon absolutely opens sever. His current Knuckles won't get there though — they're base 22/grade D, capping around 79 raw heavy even at ESV 47. That's a hard ceiling, not a "level Skill more" problem. If you want him on a sever path eventually, it'll need to come from finding or crafting a better-graded Quick weapon later, not from investing further in these Knuckles.

Given that — knuckles stay Quick/Skill/D as designed? And do we keep the 16/4 Skill/Strength split?
yeah, I think I'll run him as you built him. His name is "Prettyboy" Lloyd.

content.thinking:
Alright, confirming the character details and stats check out—the attribute distribution of 8, 10, 4, 16, 2 totals 40 points as expected. Now I'm calculating the derived stats: maximum HP comes to 108 based on the vigor modifier, and I need to finish working through movement and the remaining calculations to complete the character sheet. The attribute reallocation is free since it's just redistributing the same 40-point baseline pool for backstory reasons, so Prettyboy stays at Level 0 with Insight 1 as a starting character. Now I need to build out the full character sheet, note the weapon sever mechanics, list starting gear like the robe and token, and set up the opening scene at the calm threshold—though I should flag that Resolve 2 is quite low, giving no save bonus until it reaches 10. I'm weighing whether to flag this balance quirk to the player mid-session or save it for later—since Jake is both designer and player here, a quick heads-up makes sense rather than silently glossing over it. Now I need to create the character sheet file to track the current state, which means I should generate a fresh one since the original was lost. Writing character sheet file...

I'll set Insight and Purse/Vault to their starting values, then open the scene at the funicular station with the clerk handing over the token, robe, and key—placing Prettyboy Lloyd in that threshold moment where guests are changing and the session truly begins. I'll end on a concrete choice to pull him into the action. The values check out, so I'm ready to wrap this up.

summaries.summary: Thinking about confirming a character build decision for "Prettyboy" Lloyd.
Finalizing derived stats and calculating maximum hit points.
Verified character stats and orchestrated opening scene setup.

content.text:
Good name. Locking it in.

**Quick gut-check on the build:** Resolve 2 gives him a save-roll modifier of ⌊2÷10⌋ = **0** — identical to Resolve 8 or 9. You're not actually buying anything by dumping it *that* low; the floor doesn't bite until Resolve hits 10. If the intent was "he's mechanically fragile," 2 doesn't do more than 9 would. Your call whether that's a real gap worth flagging in the design bible or just an accepted quirk of a low-investment stat — I'll note it either way and keep moving.

**Prettyboy Lloyd — Level 0, Insight 1**

| VIG | END | STR | SKI | RES |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 10 | 4 | 16 | 2 |

- Max HP: 108 · Movement: 5m · Visceral charges: 2 · Save mod: +0
- **Metal Knuckles** ×2 (Quick / Skill D) — Light 30 / Heavy 56 at current ESV 16
- Powder Charge gimmick: load a capsule (Fast Action), land a Heavy → stopwatch check. Hit: +50% that heavy's damage + 1 Discombobulation on target. Miss: capsule wastes, he takes 1 self Blood Loss stack.
- Capsules carried: 4 · White Salts: 0 Purse / 0 Vault

Building the sheet file now.

# THE DEEP SALTS — Character Sheet (Live State)
*DM-maintained. Rewritten after any significant change. Single source of truth for HP, tracks, charges, limbs, Insight, and inventory mid-fight.*

---

## "Prettyboy" Lloyd

**Concept:** Young, athletic fighter. Blonde, blue-eyed, weary. Self-made twin knuckledusters rigged with detonating powder capsules.

**Level:** 0 · **Insight:** 1

### Attributes
| VIG | END | STR | SKI | RES |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 10 | 4 | 16 | 2 |

### Derived Stats
- **Max HP:** 108 (current: 108)
- **Movement budget:** 5m
- **Visceral charges:** 2 (current: 2/2)
- **Insanity/Influence save mod:** ⌊Resolve÷10⌋ = +0

### Weapon — Metal Knuckles (paired)
- Type: Quick · Stat/Grade: Skill / D · One-handed, paired
- Base Damage: 22
- Light = 22 + ESV×0.5 · Heavy = 44 + ESV×0.75
- **Current (ESV 16):** Light 30 · Heavy 56
- **Passive Gimmick — Powder Charge:** Load a capsule into a knuckle (Fast Action). Landing a Heavy with one loaded triggers a detonation stopwatch check:
  - **Hit window:** +50% of that heavy's raw damage; target takes 1 Discombobulation (Tick).
  - **Miss window:** capsule vents wrong; Lloyd takes 1 self-inflicted Blood Loss stack.
  - Capsule consumed either way.
- **Capsules carried:** 4/4 · restocked at hub

### Status Tracks (all currently 0/clear)
| Track | Value |
|---|---|
| Blood Loss | 0 |
| Insanity | 0 |
| Influence | 0 |
| Discombobulation | — |
| Corrosion | 0 |
| Burning | — |
| Fugue | N/A |

### Limbs
Standard, no mutations, no damage.

### White Salts
- Purse: 0
- Vault: 0

### Inventory
- Metal Knuckles ×2 (equipped)
- Powder capsules ×4
- (starter gear pending — hub intake)

### Mutations
None.

---
*Last updated: Session Zero — character creation.*
