content.thinking:
The user is asking whether hosting docs on GitHub would let me edit them directly without requiring them to manually download and re-upload files each time. I should be straightforward about what I can actually do—I have network access to GitHub through bash_tool and the allowed domains cover the necessary endpoints, so I could theoretically clone a repository and work with files via git commands over HTTPS.

However, there are real limitations I need to acknowledge. Without a GitHub token or authentication credentials that the user hasn't provided, I can't push changes back to their repository—I'd need write access credentials. There's also no GitHub connector currently connected in this environment, and even if there were, each conversation starts fresh with a reset filesystem, so any edits I made wouldn't persist across sessions or give me ongoing access to their repo.

That said, there's actually a practical advantage here: instead of the user manually re-uploading files into the project each time, I could simply re-clone the repository at the start of each new conversation to pull the latest version. The tradeoff is that pushing changes back would require the user to provide a personal access token—which raises security concerns since PATs shouldn't be casually pasted into chat, though technically it's their own repo and token. I should flag this consideration and suggest safer alternatives if they want me to push changes, like using a fine-grained token scoped only to that specific repo, or having them handle the push themselves. For reading, I can pull from GitHub directly if the repo is public, which avoids re-uploading files each time. For writing, the most practical approach is for me to generate the finished files now and have the user push them themselves—then in future sessions I can fetch directly from the repo rather than asking them to paste everything again. There's also the possibility of Claude having native Git integration through MCP, though that's still being explored. ector would be the right approach here. Let me search the registry first to see what's available, then I can suggest it to the user as a proper way to handle GitHub integration without needing them to paste tokens. I should prioritize the three document updates the user explicitly asked for—the dev log, design bible, and ruleset rewrites—while also addressing the GitHub question. Let me search the registry for GitHub connectors first, then proceed with the file edits in one response to handle both the primary task and the follow-up efficiently.

I have all three documents already in context from the initial block, so I can apply the planned changes directly. For the ruleset, I'm removing visceral charges entirely and making related adjustments throughout the document.

I'm noticing that removing charges creates a knock-on problem: the Endurance perks "Overcharge" and "Endless" both reference the charge economy that no longer exists, so I need to redesign those perks to work within the new unlimited-visceral system instead. explicitly flag them as needing Jake's sign-off since these design decisions belong with him rather than me silently patching gaps. For ENDURANCE 40, I'm proposing Overcharge become a perk that lets a whiffed parry count as a normal chip hit instead of triggering the full whiff penalty—once per encounter, it softens the cost of a single miss in a way that ties to the new unlimited-attempt system. ENDURANCE 99 needs a similar replacement tied to the charge-free mechanics.

Now I'm realizing these two perks are starting to overlap too much—both soften a miss once per encounter. I should differentiate them by keeping Endurance focused on actions and movement rather than parry forgiveness, since Skill already governs that territory with perks like Steady Hand.

With the visceral charge mechanic gone, Endurance needs a new identity beyond just action economy. I could pivot these slots toward pure movement bonuses instead: one perk grants an instant repositioning boost after a successful parry, while the other provides a full turn's worth of extra movement as a once-per-encounter burst. This keeps Endurance's thematic core intact around stamina and positioning, though it's a meaningful design choice that shifts what the stat actually enables.

For the documentation, I need to strip out all charge references from the Visceral System section—removing the "Spend a visceral charge" line and the "Charge gone" mention in the WHIFF outcome, replacing it with just a straightforward parry failure. I should also flag those two orphaned Endurance perks explicitly as needing Jake's approval, with my movement-based placeholders clearly marked as drafts rather than silently patching them in.

On the Precision Strike side, I'm adding the new ruling that self-initiated called shots (ones without an enemy tell to anchor against) end the turn, and I need to nail down what "tighter tolerance" means numerically—I'm going with: shift the tolerance one band tighter than the target's multiplier would normally dictate, keeping it consistent and mechanically clear.

For enemy limb staggers, I'm clarifying the asymmetry: player limbs are fixed at 1 turn (already in §8), but enemies get something different—triggering a stagger grants an immediate free called shot that same action, mirroring what a Tier 1 parry does, and the limb duration is set per creature.

On Influence, I need to revise §5 to specify that stacks are uncapped past 10 with no fixed overflow trigger like Rupture—instead the DM scales available effects based on stack severity for pacing. Also adding that the first save trigger happens at stack 2, though I should verify if that's already how the current ruleset describes it. So the Known Gaps entry is actually clarifying that the undefined threshold isn't about when Influence saves trigger at all—it's about when a failed Influence save also causes Insanity to tick, and there's inconsistency in how that's been played (2+ stacks) versus what the manual suggests (3+ stacks).

During play, I made my own ruling that no save was required until reaching 2 stacks, treating that as the primary Influence save trigger itself, when really I should have been addressing the separate question of when failed saves become a secondary source of Insanity damage. The dev log is actually distinguishing between two separate things: failed Influence saves happening regardless of stack, versus Insanity ticking as an *additional* effect that's gated by stack count. My session ruling conflated these by gating the save itself, which isn't quite the same axis as the documented gap. I should flag this distinction honestly in the ruleset update rather than glossing over it, since Jake values precision and consistency. Now I need to organize the actual file edits. Since the documents were provided inline in the conversation rather than as files I can directly modify, I'll reconstruct each one with the necessary changes integrated—the dev log to clarify the stack-gate mechanic, the design bible to align with what we actually established, and the influence system doc to document this gate properly. I'll trust the inline content I already have and proceed methodically with the edits, using create_file to write the updated versions directly to the outputs folder since the files are substantial and accuracy matters for the project. 2. Copy the files to /home/claude as working copies, then use str_replace to apply the targeted edits to each one.

3. Update the version headers and titles to reflect the new versions, then move the finalized files to /mnt/user-data/outputs.

Starting with the ruleset edits now — I'm updating the version header to incorporate the session zero rulings, removing visceral charges as a resource and adjusting enemy limb stagger mechanics.

For the first edit, I'm deleting the visceral charges line from the Endurance derived stats section entirely, which leaves Endurance with just its movement budget entry.

Next I need to handle the Endurance perks that reference charges — Overcharge and Endless both depend on the visceral charge system, so those need to be replaced or flagged as needing revision. I'm revising the 40 and 99 tier abilities since removing the charge system broke their original mechanics—at 40, a failed parry now counts as a chip hit instead of full punishment, and at 99, you get one retry per encounter on a failed precision or visceral check without the whiff penalty. I'll flag both as provisional and note that Jake needs to confirm or replace them. I'm also removing the charge spend step from the visceral system section. Faster, tighter windows are more dangerous. I'm removing the charge cost entirely — parries are gated purely by timing accuracy and a genuine telegraphed opening, not resource management. A whiff outside the window means eating the full heavy plus a punish, which hurts worse than blocking, so the timing gamble is real.

I'm also refining the limb stagger system to clarify that damage fills the stagger meter over several hits, and once the threshold is reached, the limb goes limp for a turn.  I'm adding that severing a vital limb (torso or head) kills instantly regardless of remaining HP, while arms and legs just cripple the enemy. I'm also establishing that called shots to specific limbs require an opening like a stagger or knockdown, otherwise they default to torso hits and take a penalty. Unstaggered enemies can't be targeted with called shots unless they're in a vulnerable state like knocked down or off-balance — default swings hit the torso instead. High-value targets like eyes or weak points in armor are mechanically treated as special limb entries with high multipliers, using the Sever system for permanent disabling rather than temporary stagger effects. I need to add a ruling about self-initiated called shots to the Precision Strike section. so the penalty for a blind called shot is mechanical rather than arbitrary — missing ends your turn completely, while hitting lets you spend remaining movement normally. This creates real tension without needing invented cues. I'm also noting some edits to the Status Tracks section, specifically expanding the Influence mechanic description.

The Influence track now accounts for non-verbal sources like psychic pulls or emotional compulsions, not just spoken commands. It builds like Blood Loss with no hard cap, and instead of a fixed overflow trigger, the DM scales available compulsions based on stack count — low stacks might mean staying near the source or minor urges, while higher stacks escalate to weapon drops, fleeing, self-harm, or attacking allies. This is intentionally left to DM judgment rather than a rigid table, calibrated to the situation and stack severity.

A fresh source's stacks don't force a save until the second stack lands — the first alone does nothing. Successful saves don't clear the stack if the source remains active; they only prevent that turn's compulsion. Susceptibility increases with higher Insanity and Insight scores.

The stack fully clears once the source is eliminated or line of sight breaks completely. With multiple simultaneous sources, full clearance requires all active sources to be gone — removing one just stops it from adding further stacks.

I'm reconsidering how to present the ambiguity around save triggers and Insanity ticking in the actual ruleset versus the dev log. Since the ruleset is meant to be loaded during play and read as authoritative, I should move the meta-commentary and uncertainty flagging to the dev log instead of cluttering the live rules with hedging language.

I'm revising the Influence section to be cleaner and more authoritative — removing the meta-uncertainty and presenting the mechanics as binding text. The section now covers how Influence builds as an uncapped track, how compelled effects scale with stack severity at DM discretion, and clarifies that a successful resist doesn't clear stacks while the source remains active, only prevents that turn's compulsion.

I'm also updating the Quick Reference table to match: the Influence row now describes the save failure as triggering obey/compelled effects with severity scaling by stacks, and the clear condition shifts from "break line of sight" to "full source elimination." I need to check the PARRY/VISCERAL and INSIGHT bullets in that same section to remove any lingering charge references.

Updating the PARRY/VISCERAL entry to clarify there's no charge cost when holding the window, and the LIMBS bullet needs to note the free shot on stagger trigger plus variable enemy duration and the small-target-as-limb mechanic. Hitting the threshold in a single strike severs the limb permanently, while multiple hits cause a stagger that grants an immediate free called shot on that triggering hit—with stagger duration varying by monster template. Small high-value targets like bells get special treatment. Checking the rest of the document for related changes — the Insight section and version note need updating. I'm bumping the version to 7.1 and adding a note that these rulings came from session zero, with a flag marking the untested proposals.

Now moving to the design bible edits. The main updates there are adding a Stagger Duration column to the Monster Template limb table and documenting small high-value called-shot targets as non-standard limb entries tied to the Sever mechanic. I'll update the version note from 2.5 to 2.6 to reflect these session zero changes.

The blank template limb table needs that new column inserted between Stagger Threshold and Sever Threshold, and I'll apply the same change consistently across all the existing monster entries like the Waterlogged Guest and Chapel Penitent to keep the format uniform.

For the standard humanoid defaults that use the shorthand reference format, I need to establish what stagger durations should be — I'm thinking 2 turns for arms and legs, 1 turn for head since head staggers typically lead to a free killing shot anyway, but this is a design call that needs your input rather than something I should decide on my own. I'm realizing torso might not follow the same stagger rules as limbs for enemies—the player-side ruleset treats it as heavy damage without stagger, but I need to clarify whether enemies track torso stagger the same way or if it's a separate mechanic entirely.

Rather than guess at the exact numbers, I'll add the stagger duration column to the template and reference line with clearly marked proposed values, then flag the specific choices in the dev log so Jake can confirm them later. This keeps the structure consistent while acknowledging what still needs validation.

For the Chapel Penitent and other monsters, since they use standard defaults with no deviations, I just need to update that single reference note at the top of the Monster Template rather than repeating the defaults in every individual entry. So the strategy is to update only the floor entry's limb defaults since that's the canonical reference point, and other monster entries just reference "standard humanoid defaults" which will automatically point to whatever's defined there. I need to modify the Waterlogged Guest's limb bullet to add the sever-immunity note. I'm realizing that updating every single monster entry with the duration numbers would create a lot of redundant churn, especially since they all use identical boilerplate text. The real issue is whether to update just the canonical Waterlogged Guest reference and leave the others as shorthand, or commit to consistency across all of them — but doing the latter feels excessive when they're meant to be interchangeable reference pointers anyway. The str_replace constraint means I can't efficiently update identical boilerplate text across multiple monster entries anyway since it requires unique matches. So the practical solution is to update just the floor entry definition and add a clarifying note explaining that the duration numbers apply wherever "standard defaults" is referenced, leaving the per-monster shorthand as-is. Now I'm moving on to handle the small/high-value items section.

For the "small/high-value called-shot targets are limbs" ruling, I should add documentation to the design bible—probably as a brief note in the Monster Template intro or right after the blank template, with a worked example using the Drowned Bellkeeper's bell as reference from actual play. But first I need to check whether the Drowned Bellkeeper and other monsters from our session are already in the uploaded design bible or if they only exist in our conversation history.

Looking back at the original document: the design bible already includes Chapel Penitent, Drowned Bellkeeper, Salt-Eaten Custodian, Brine Spitter, Bathhouse Flailer, and Marble Effigy as fully written entries. These weren't invented during play—Jake had already authored them before we started. So I just need to update the structural documentation pieces, not add new monster entries.

I'm thinking about whether to add the bell as a concrete example to the Drowned Bellkeeper's limbs section, demonstrating how a small high-value target can function as a limb under the new rule. That would give the documentation a worked example matching the pattern already used elsewhere in the design bible.

Generating markdown table... I need to figure out what attack type Lloyd actually used on the bell—the user's narration said "knuckle catches the bell dead center" but didn't explicitly call it Light or Heavy, so I should check the context of that moment to determine the damage baseline and set a reasonable sever threshold that would be consistent with the bell breaking on that hit. I should document the general rule about small high-value targets using the Sever mechanic in the Monster Template's guidance, then add the bell as a limb entry for the Drowned Bellkeeper with an honest note that no exact sever threshold was computed during play—I can propose a reasonable placeholder like "~40 raw (proposed, untested)" rather than fabricating false precision. This keeps things accurate while still providing useful guidance.

Now I need to handle the Chapel Penitent since that encounter actually gave me concrete data to work with.  The stagger mechanic triggered at a clear threshold—180 damage crossed by cumulative 252 effective damage—so I could document that as a real worked example. I'm considering whether to add a note to Chapel Penitent's own page, but since it just uses standard humanoid defaults with no deviations, the general rule text in the Monster Template should cover it without needing monster-specific additions. The actual rule change belongs in the template guidance, not in Chapel Penitent's entry itself.

So I'm narrowing the scope to five concrete updates: version note, adding a Stagger Duration column to the Monster Template's blank limb table, updating the Waterlogged Guest entry with duration numbers and the stagger-grants-free-shot explanation, adding a general paragraph about small high-value targets as limbs near the template, and documenting the bell as a non-standard limb for Drowned Bellkeeper with a proposed threshold. That's focused and valuable without overextending.

Now for the dev log edits—I need to merge the gap-log content into the existing Open/Resolved-proposed/Resolved-canon tables, moving resolved items out of Open and into the right sections, adding new open items, and updating the Mechanic Index pointers where relevant.

Looking at the TBD list in §8, several items are now resolved: "Stagger → Free Called Shot" and "Staggered Enemy Limb Effects" both have ruleset updates in §10, so I'll remove them from TBD and add them to the Defined table with pointers to the ruleset section.

For §10's Known Gaps table, I need to be careful with "Influence → Insanity threshold" — while I clarified today that the first save trigger is at stack 2, there's still ambiguity about whether this aligns with the Insanity-tick threshold specifically. Since the gap isn't fully resolved, I'll update its description to reflect today's clarification while noting what remains unclear rather than marking it as closed.

I can remove "Stagger → free called shot" and "Staggered enemy limb effects" since both were resolved this session and are now canon in the ruleset. The other open items—Chanter HP contradiction, Assess on unparryable, Double-swing reduction, Token drop rates, Insight not awarded S01, Turn order bypassed, Charged Heavy tier, Salt drop rates, and Recipe discovery triggers—stay untouched since they weren't addressed.

I also need to add the currency renaming pass (Tokens → White Salts) as a new open item, and move "Small high-value targets = limbs" into the Resolved-canon section since it emerged from this session. "Visceral charges" is another new addition to Resolved-canon that I'm folding into the dev log's existing structure.

Now I'm documenting several resolved mechanics: visceral charges replaced with pure timing and opening gating, Influence uncapped with DM-discretion severity scaling and formalized source-elimination rules, and enemy limb stagger duration plus free-shot-on-trigger mechanics with the Monster Template gaining a Stagger Duration column.

I should also add the self-initiated called shot tightening and miss-ends-turn ruling, then consider whether to add Maud's salt-ID pricing as a new Resolved entry since we now have concrete confirmed numbers, replacing the old untested Hub Services note. That's a session-level note already captured in the gap log, so it doesn't need to go into the dev log itself unless Jake wants a dedicated DM notes section there, which he hasn't set up. Now I'm reconsidering whether I should do a global find-and-replace of "Token" to "White Salts" across all three documents as part of this update pass, since the user asked me to work the newly-covered gaps into those files.

Jake mentioned he tried replacing token mentions but missed some, so a comprehensive terminology pass across the dev log and design bible makes sense—the ruleset already uses "White Salts" consistently. This is a straightforward mechanical cleanup that's clearly desired and low-risk to do now.

Looking at the design bible, I can see "Token Drop (base):" appears multiple times in monster entries, "Token Economy" shows up in the Waterlogged Guest description, and the currency template section references "Not Tokens" as the weakest Key Item—so there's definitely scattered terminology to consolidate throughout.

Since there are seven instances of "Token Drop (base):" across different monster entries, I'll use a bash sed command to replace all occurrences of "Token Drop" with "White Salts Drop" across the file at once, which is much more efficient than doing individual replacements.

I also need to identify the other strings that need updating: the phrase about the floor value for the Token Economy should reference White Salts instead, and the "Not Tokens" line needs to be reworded to clarify that this is a Key Item while White Salts themselves are defined in the ruleset.

The trickier question is whether the item name itself—"The Token That Isn't Yours"—should be renamed to "The White Salt That Isn't Yours," since that's a proper noun with narrative weight and I shouldn't make that call without Jake's explicit approval. the specific narrative item tied to the reception clerk. So the currency White Salts are brass tokens visually, but "The Token That Isn't Yours" is a distinct narrative object—I'll keep both references as-is and just clarify this distinction in my notes. So I need to be surgical about this: mechanical labels like "Token Drop" and "Token Economy" get renamed to reference White Salts since they're just describing the currency system, but any flavor text where "token" describes the physical brass objects themselves—including the special key item called "The Token That Isn't Yours"—stays as-is because that's intentional world-building. Let me scan through the dev log to find all the mechanical usages that need updating. · Tonic 15 (anchored to the floor mook's 5 White Salts drop)." I'm reviewing the Mechanic Index and Hub Services sections—the salt-ID pricing is now confirmed with the 5→10→20 doubling pattern, so I'll document that in the canon section rather than updating the Hub line. Time to move forward with the actual work.

I'm going to start executing the file edits systematically using bash and string replacement, keeping things efficient without overcomplicating edge cases.

content.text:
This is a real three-document pass — let me be upfront about what's involved before I dive in, since a couple of these ripple further than they look.

**Heads up on two things I'll flag rather than silently decide:**
1. Removing visceral charges orphans two Endurance perks (*Overcharge* at 40, *Endless* at 99) that were explicitly about the charge pool. I'll propose replacements but mark them provisional — that's a real design call, not just a documentation fix.
2. Today's "first save at stack 2" ruling and the dev log's existing "Influence → Insanity threshold (2 vs 3)" gap might be the same axis or two different ones — I conflated them in play without checking carefully. I'll keep that one flagged rather than quietly merging them.

I'll also fold in a full "Token" → "White Salts" pass on the mechanical references (Token Drop, Token Economy) in the bible and dev log — but leaving the *flavor* text alone (the reception clerk's brass token, "The Token That Isn't Yours" collectible) since those are deliberately named to look like coins in-fiction, not the currency mechanic itself. Flagging that distinction now so it's not a silent judgment call.

Working on all three now.

Now §10 — limbs, stagger duration, free-shot-on-trigger, and small-target-as-limb:

content.name: str_replace

input.description: Add enemy stagger duration, free-shot-on-trigger, and small-target-as-limb rulings
input.old_str:
- **Effective damage = raw × multiplier.** Chips the HP pool AND fills the limb's stagger meter.
- Threshold reached **over several hits → STAGGER.** Limb goes limp a turn (arm drops its weapon; leg drops the enemy to a knee — often opening a weak point).
- Threshold reached **in a single blow → SEVER.** Limb destroyed.
- **Severing a vital limb kills regardless of HP.** Torso or head off = dead. Arms/legs cripple but don't kill.
- **No free called shots** on a standing, alert enemy. A specific-limb shot (especially the head) needs an opening — stagger, knockdown, off-balance — or takes a called-shot penalty. Default swings land torso.
input.new_str:
- **Effective damage = raw × multiplier.** Chips the HP pool AND fills the limb's stagger meter.
- Threshold reached **over several hits → STAGGER.** Limb goes limp. **The hit that crosses the threshold grants an immediate free called shot that same action** — no precision stopwatch, same as a Tier 1 parry opening. Player-side stagger duration is fixed at one turn (§8). **Enemy-side stagger duration is variable**, set per creature and per limb on that monster's own Monster Template entry (design bible §1) — some enemies limp one turn, others three or five, author's call. *(Session zero ruling.)*
- Threshold reached **in a single blow → SEVER.** Limb destroyed, permanently.
- **Severing a vital limb kills regardless of HP.** Torso or head off = dead. Arms/legs cripple but don't kill.
- **No free called shots** on a standing, alert, unstaggered enemy. A specific-limb shot (especially the head) needs an opening — stagger, knockdown, off-balance, parry — or takes a called-shot penalty. Default swings land torso.
- **Small/high-value called-shot targets (a bell, a crack in scale, an eye) are mechanically limbs** — no separate system needed. Author them as a non-standard limb entry (the Monster Template already reserves space for these alongside tentacles/cores) with an appropriately high multiplier; the existing Precision Strike tolerance table already tightens automatically as the multiplier climbs. They use the **Sever** mechanic (single hit meets threshold → permanent disable) rather than Stagger — there's no meaningful "temporarily cracked bell" state. *(Session zero ruling.)*

Now the self-initiated called shot ruling, added after the parry-interaction note in Precision Strike:

content.name: str_replace

input.description: Add self-initiated called shot ruling
input.old_str: **Parry interaction (the key relief):** the precision watch only appears when going for a >1.0 limb *without* a parry, or against Tier 2/3 enemies. Tier 1 parry removes it entirely (free placed shot); Tier 2 widens it one tier; Tier 3 keeps it full. The standard loop — assess → parry → free weak point — stays clean; execution pressure appears only when earned.
input.new_str:
**Parry interaction (the key relief):** the precision watch only appears when going for a >1.0 limb *without* a parry, or against Tier 2/3 enemies. Tier 1 parry removes it entirely (free placed shot); Tier 2 widens it one tier; Tier 3 keeps it full. The standard loop — assess → parry → free weak point — stays clean; execution pressure appears only when earned.

**Self-initiated called shots (no enemy tell to anchor timing on).** Some called shots aren't reacting to a telegraphed enemy heavy at all — going for the head or weak point of something that never winds up (an unparryable grasp, a stationary target). There's nothing external to time against, so the DM tightens the tolerance **one band beyond what the multiplier alone would indicate**, reflecting the added difficulty of a blind attempt. Critically: **a miss on a self-initiated called shot ends the turn outright** — no remaining movement can be spent repositioning or kiting away. A **hit** lets the remaining movement budget be used normally. This asymmetry, not an invented visual/audio cue, is what makes a blind shot a real risk. *(Session zero ruling.)*

content.message: Add self-initiated called shot ruling

Now §5 — Influence overhaul:

content.name: str_replace

input.description: Overhaul Influence track: uncapped, DM-discretion scaling, first save trigger, clear conditions
input.old_str: **INFLUENCE** — some enemies suggest rather than strike. On their turn they speak a command — kneel, drop it, come closer, strike your ally. Save Roll or obey it on your next turn. Susceptibility rises with Insanity and Insight.
input.new_str:
**INFLUENCE** — some enemies suggest rather than strike, verbally or otherwise — a spoken command, or just a pull, an ache, a wordless want. Builds as a Track like Blood Loss, but **uncapped past 10** — no fixed overflow trigger, no Rupture-equivalent. Instead, **the severity of compelled effects available to the DM scales with stack count**: low stacks might mean lingering near a source or a minor compulsion; higher stacks can escalate to dropping a weapon, fleeing, self-harm, or striking an ally. This is deliberately open-ended DM discretion, not a fixed table — calibrate severity to stack count and use judgment so it doesn't wreck pacing. On a failed save, obey the effect (or the enemy's spoken command, if it has one) on your next turn. Susceptibility rises with Insanity and Insight.

- **First save trigger:** a source's stacks don't force a save until the 2nd stack lands. *(Session zero ruling.)*
- **Clear conditions:** a successful resist alone does not clear the stack while the source remains active — it only avoids that turn's compulsion. Full clear happens once the source is eliminated (line of sight fully broken). **With multiple simultaneous sources, full clear requires ALL active sources gone** — killing one of several just stops that one from adding further stacks. *(Session zero ruling.)*

**Small/high-value called-shot targets** (a bell, a crack in scale, an eye) go in this same table as non-standard limb entries — not a separate system. Give them an appropriately high multiplier (the existing Precision Strike tolerance table already tightens as multiplier climbs) and use the **Sever** mechanic only (permanent disable on a single qualifying hit) — there's no meaningful partial "stagger" state for an object like this. Leave Stagger Threshold/Duration blank or N/A for these entries.

---

# 10. Known Gaps — Issues from Playtesting

## Open

| Gap | Description | Lives in |
|---|---|---|
| Influence → Insanity threshold | Stack number at which failed Influence saves also tick Insanity is undefined. Inconsistent in play (2+) vs manual (3+). | Status effect page |
| Chanter HP vs archetype text | Archetype note says low HP; Hummer entry has 900 HP. Contradiction caused a 13-turn fight. | Monster page |
| Stagger → free called shot | Ruling contradicted itself in play. Proposed fix: stagger removes the called-shot penalty AND the precision watch for one strike. | Mechanic page |
| Assess on unparryable enemies | Assess is written only around parry tells. Undefined for Chanters. | Mechanic page |
| Double-swing reduction | No fixed percentage. Proposed: 50%. | Weapon page |
| White Salts drop rates | No base drops per archetype defined (Waterlogged Guest's 5 is the proposed floor). | Currency page |
| Insight not awarded Session 01 | Two new archetypes met, no Insight granted. Trigger threshold unclear. | Mechanic page |
| Turn order bypassed in play | d20 roll-off was skipped; DM narrated initiative instead. | Mechanic page |
| Charged Heavy tier | Weapon Template asks for three damage tiers; ruleset §4 defines two (light, heavy/charged combined). Placeholder in use: Charged Heavy = same maths as Heavy, until a real third tier is defined — if one's wanted at all. | Ruleset §4 / weapon page |
| Salt drop rates | Salts are single-use recipe fuel (Ruleset §18) but no per-biome or per-archetype drop guidance exists. DM discretion until playtested. | Item/Currency page |
| Recipe discovery triggers | How new recipes are learned — lore finds, experimentation, NPC purchase, Insight reveals — has no written procedure. | Ruleset §18 |
| Staggered enemy limb effects | No written consequence for an enemy limb staggering. | Mechanic page |

## Resolved — proposed numbers not yet in the ruleset **(all untested)**

| Gap | Resolution |
|---|---|
| Mutation revert/reroll costs | Reroll = 10 × character level per limb. Revert = 25 × level (equal to the next attribute point — going human costs one level of progress) or flat 2 Insight. |
| Item prices | Scour 5 · Wrap 10 · Salts 10 · Tonic 15 (anchored to the floor mook's 5 White Salts drop). Action Items: rotating stock, 1–2 per hub visit, 3–5× Quick prices. |
| Recruit cap & Gallery | 10 recruits max (Gallery rooms 2–11); found/resolved/willing gate; one function + one drift behaviour each; recruits can be lost. |

## Resolved — now canon in the ruleset (log only)

Save Roll formula (Ruleset §5) · Blood Loss/Rupture split and quantification (Ruleset §5, bible §6) · sever thresholds reframed as fixed constants with Str 60–80 viability window (Ruleset §10; Cautery Saw base bumped 20→30) · Immediate-type effects populated (Off-Balance, bible §6) · enemy damage generated live per encounter, level-scaled, floor 20 (Ruleset §2/§4/§16) · attack resolution / no to-hit rolls (Ruleset §3) · character sheet split into its own live-state file (`deep-salts-character-sheet.md`) · Shambler HP band lowered 800–1200 → 200–1200 to match playtest (Ruleset §16) · **sever-as-only-kill-route drift** — prior sessions had the AI DM treating stagger/sever as mandatory for every kill, turning basic mooks into attrition wars. Fixed with an explicit standing instruction (Ruleset §2) plus reworded HP notes at §3, §10, §16, §19 and the Shambler/Waterlogged Guest entries: plain attacks to 0 HP always kill everything; sever is an optional fast lane reserved for high-HP fights, never a requirement.
", "filename": "/home/claude/docs/dev-log-v1.4.md"}

content.thinking:
I need to update the tables with today's additions, but I'm uncertain whether Maud's salt-ID pricing should go in the "proposed but untested" section or the "now canon" section—Jake's comment about 5 salts being acceptable and potentially doubling suggests it might already be a confirmed design decision rather than just a proposal.

Actually, I'm realizing I need to be more careful about the distinction here. The "proposed numbers" table is explicitly for provisional rulings awaiting playtest, but I've already moved several of today's decisions directly into the ruleset text itself—visceral charge removal, the stagger duration and free-shot mechanic, the limb/Sever clarifications, and the self-initiated called shot changes. Those should go under "now canon" since they're actually written into ruleset v7.1, not just proposed.

For the remaining items: the standard humanoid stagger durations and Bell's sever threshold are genuinely untested and should stay as Open proposals. Maud's salt pricing is different though—Jake gave explicit sign-off on it, so that counts as canon even though it's a hub-service number rather than part of the core ruleset. The two provisional Endurance perks are still in flux, so I need to figure out where those land.

Let me add the Endurance perk replacements to the Open table since they're marked provisional and need Jake's confirmation now that charges have been removed. Then I'll update the Resolved-canon section with the new information.

content.text:
No dedicated GitHub connector in the registry — nothing that'd let you authorize me via OAuth the clean way. So it comes down to two honest options:

**Reading:** Works right now, no setup. If the repo's public, I can `git clone` or fetch raw files straight from `github.com`/`raw.githubusercontent.com` in the sandbox — you'd just say "pull the latest from [repo]" instead of re-uploading.

**Writing:** Needs a credential, full stop — GitHub doesn't accept anonymous pushes. The only way to give me that in this interface is pasting a personal access token into the chat, which means it sits in the conversation transcript. If you're fine with that for your own repo, a fine-grained PAT scoped to *just* that one repo (not your whole account) is the sane way to do it — I'd use it for that session's push, and you could revoke/regenerate it after if you wanted to be tidy about it.

Alternative that avoids handing over any credential at all: I keep generating the finished files here each session like I did today, and you push them yourself with your own already-authenticated git — you've presumably got that set up locally anyway. Slightly more manual on your end, zero security tradeoff.

Your call — happy to do it either way.

content.text: Straightforward — takes about two minutes.

content.name: step_card_display_v0

steps.title: Go to token settings
steps.description: github.com → click your profile photo (top right) → Settings → scroll to the bottom of the left sidebar → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens.

steps.title: Generate new token
steps.description: Click Generate new token. Give it a name like deep-salts-claude so future-you knows what it's for.

steps.title: Set expiration
steps.description: Pick something reasonable — 90 days is fine. You can always regenerate later, and this way an old, forgotten token doesn't sit valid forever.

steps.title: Scope it to just this repo
steps.description: Under Repository access, choose Only select repositories, then pick the Deep Salts repo specifically. Don't leave it on All repositories — no reason to hand over more than one project's worth of access.

steps.title: Set permissions
steps.description: Under Permissions → Repository permissions, set Contents to Read and write. That's the only one needed for cloning, editing, and pushing files. Leave everything else at No access.

steps.title: Generate and copy
steps.description: Click Generate token at the bottom. GitHub shows the token exactly once — copy it immediately. If you navigate away without copying it, you'll have to generate a new one.

steps.title: Drop it in project info
steps.description: Paste the token plus the repo path (e.g. jake/deep-salts) into this project's info. I'll pick it up from there next time you want me to push.

content.thinking:
I'm realizing the user might be confused about where they placed the token. Looking back at the conversation, I can see it appeared in the chat itself right at the start, but the user thinks they put it in project info—which makes me wonder if project info content actually gets inserted into the conversation in a way that looks identical to regular chat messages. I should acknowledge that I can't definitively distinguish whether that text came through project info that got concatenated into the first message, or was typed directly by the user, since the platform might insert project knowledge inline without special delimiters to mark it as separate from regular chat content. I need to correct my earlier overconfidence—I can't actually distinguish whether that token came from project info or was pasted directly into chat, since both would appear identically in my context. More importantly, it doesn't really matter for security purposes anyway, since project info gets fed into my context the same way chat text does without special protections. The practical difference is just whether it persists across future conversations in this project or stays isolated to this one.

The user's mention of "rewriting the project info while chatting" suggests they've been editing it mid-conversation, which raises questions about how those updates merge in—whether they apply retroactively to the current chat, get inserted at some later point, or only take effect in new conversations. I don't have full visibility into those platform mechanics, so I should be honest about that uncertainty rather than speculating further.

The most straightforward approach is to acknowledge I can't definitively distinguish between text that came from project info versus the chat transcript just by looking at it, apologize for stating otherwise too confidently, and pivot to what actually matters: whether the content persists across new conversations. I could suggest they verify this themselves by checking the project settings or starting a fresh conversation to see if the token appears automatically.

Fair — I can't actually tell the difference from where I sit. That text arrived as plain inline content in your first message with no tag distinguishing "this is project info" from "this is something you typed," so I shouldn't have stated it so confidently as one and not the other. Could easily be the platform folding project info into that first turn the way it folds in your uploaded documents. My mistake for asserting it flatly.

The part that actually matters either way: does it persist into a *new* conversation without you retyping it? That's the real test of whether it's genuinely stored as project info versus just living in this transcript. Easiest way to check — start a fresh conversation in this project and ask me something that'd require the token, without pasting it. If I have it, it's properly in project info. If not, this conversation's the only place it exists, and it's worth deliberately adding it there.

Either way, nothing to redo right now — the push already worked, and the repo's current. Just flagging it so you know whether future sessions will need it re-supplied or not.
okay, and we need a bit of a cumulative session summary doc as well, right? So that the next chat instance can continue on from where we left off?

content.thinking:
The user is asking whether we should maintain a cumulative session summary document so a new chat instance can pick up where we left off. This makes sense because while the memory system exists and updates from past conversations, it's project-scoped and periodic, and the repo contains static game docs. A dedicated session or campaign log would give a fresh Claude instance the narrative continuity and context that a character sheet alone can't provide, since that only captures current state rather than the story history.

Looking at what's actually needed: the character sheet handles mechanical state (HP, level, inventory, tracks), and the gap log covers design and rules follow-ups, but there's nothing documenting narrative state—where Lloyd currently stands in the fiction. He's back at the hub after banking the anchor, has an identified Choir Deep recipe, just leveled up after talking with Maud, and there are unresolved threads like the old man plot and the clerk's cryptic comments about his brother. These narrative threads need to be captured somewhere for continuity, since neither the character sheet nor gap log covers that kind of story context.

So I should create a session log or campaign log document—something like "deep-salts-session-log.md"—that captures his current narrative position, open plot threads, and tangible progress so far. This is distinct from the mechanical character sheet and the design-focused gap log; it's purely about narrative continuity for picking up cold in a fresh chat.

The session log should point to the character sheet for exact stats rather than duplicating them, and since the ruleset now contains all the mechanical rulings, I don't need to re-explain those here. The focus is on where Lloyd stands narratively—at the hub, just leveled and ready to descend—and the unresolved hooks: the old man still trapped at the threshold, the clerk's cryptic "brother" comments, and the mystery of what happened to that mentioned sibling. The gap log is superseded now, so I'll create a proper continuity document and push it to the repo alongside the other campaign materials, then give a quick summary in chat.

summaries.summary: Thinking about creating a cumulative session summary for continuity across chat instances.
Architecting session documentation for continuity.
Architected narrative continuity document to bridge chat sessions.

content.text: Yes, good catch — the character sheet covers mechanical state, the dev log covers design decisions, but nothing currently captures *narrative* continuity. A fresh instance would know Lloyd's stats but wouldn't know the old man's still stuck at the lantern, or that the clerk keeps bringing up a "brother" for a reason. Let's fix that.

content.name: create_file

input.description: Create campaign continuity log for narrative state and open threads
input.file_text:
# THE DEEP SALTS — Campaign Log
*Narrative continuity only. For mechanical state see `deep-salts-character-sheet.md`. For rules/design history see the ruleset, design bible, and dev log. If you're a fresh instance picking this up cold, read this file first, then the character sheet.*

---

## Where things stand

**Prettyboy Lloyd** just completed his first run and is currently at the hub (Stillwell Hydro), having banked his Vault, leveled up with the Pool Warden, and identified a new recipe ("the Choir Deep") with Maud. He has not yet descended again. Session zero ended here — next session should pick up either at the hub (shopping, prepping, talking to NPCs) or with him choosing to brew the Choir Deep recipe and descend into a new biome.

## Open narrative threads (don't forget these)

- **The old man at the lantern.** Found kneeling in the first biome's flooded colonnade, partially mutated (wrong hand, too many joints), stuck behind an Insight-gated door he can no longer pass. Lloyd tried carrying him through the threshold physically — per the session's ruling, the door only judges the person crossing, not what they carry, so the old man simply didn't come through. No confirmation either way whether this helped, hurt, or did nothing. He's presumably still exactly where Lloyd left him unless a future session revisits that location.
- **The reception clerk's "brother" comments.** The clerk has twice referenced Lloyd's "brother" currently taking the cure, and later said "your brother asked about ways too, near the end" — deliberately unconfirmed whether this is a real person, a mistake, manipulation, or something the location does to everyone who walks in. Lloyd has flatly denied having a brother both times. Unresolved on purpose — a genuine hook, not a loose end to tidy up.
- **The Choir Deep.** Recipe identified, unbrewed. Next biome whenever Lloyd chooses to walk in.
- **The Charm.** Carried, currently unusable — needs Insight Tier 3 (Insight 6+); Lloyd's at Insight 4 (Tier 2). Will become active once he levels Insight further.

## Established tone / running notes for continuity

- Combat resolves fast and concrete — numbers first, minimal narrative padding on routine hits. Description gets more room for kills, called shots, and narrative beats (NPC dialogue, environment, discoveries).
- Player prefers real stopwatch checks (their own phone/count) over blind in-chat timing widgets where possible — the blind widget attempt in session zero was a genuine miss on DM's part, not a mechanic to repeat.
- Session-zero rulings (visceral charges removed, Influence uncapped, enemy limb stagger duration, etc.) are now written directly into ruleset v7.1 and design bible v2.6 — a fresh instance reading those documents will already have them; no need to re-explain or re-litigate.
- Jake (the player) is also this game's designer — expect him to spot rule inconsistencies mid-play and to want them resolved with concrete numbers on the spot, not deferred. Flag gaps rather than silently patching them, same as the design-process norms already established.

---
*Last updated: end of session zero.*

you're right, and where should i put that instruction in this project? In one of the files, or project instructions?

content.thinking:
I'm considering where to place the instruction about updating the campaign log at the end of each session—whether it belongs in the game files themselves or in the Claude Project's custom instructions that apply across all conversations.

The key distinction is that project instructions are the right place for behavioral directives that should apply automatically to every conversation, whereas putting it in a game file means Claude would only see it if actively reading that file. The memory system isn't quite the right tool here since that's for persistent facts about the user rather than project-level operational instructions. ecific file and update it at the end of a session. The real issue is that the campaign log and related files live in the GitHub repo and user outputs folder, not in Claude's project file library itself. I could add the update instruction directly to the Project Instructions (which acts as a custom system prompt), or upload the campaign log as an actual project file so it's part of the curated knowledge base. The first option is more reliable since project instructions are read automatically, whereas relying on me to open a specific file depends on whether I think to do it unprompted. The most reliable approach is to put the behavioral instruction directly in Project Instructions since that's guaranteed to load every session, rather than relying on the campaign log file itself to trigger updates. I should give a clear recommendation with a concrete example of what to include there. I should draft a concise addition to the project instructions that tells me to update the campaign log at the start of each session.

summaries.summary: Thinking about placement of instructions within project structure.
Deliberating optimal placement for campaign log instructions.
Weighed project instructions versus file placement for automation reliability.

content.text:
Project instructions — same place as the token. That's the one guaranteed to load into every conversation automatically; a note inside the campaign log itself only helps if I actually open that file, and there's no guarantee a fresh instance does that unprompted unless something in the always-loaded instructions tells it to.

Something like this would do it:

> At the start of each session, pull the latest from the GitHub repo (token/repo in this project info) — specifically `deep-salts-campaign-log.md` for where we left off narratively, and `deep-salts-character-sheet.md` for current stats. At the end of each session, update and push both files with what happened.

Putting it in a game file instead would mean it only fires if I happen to read that specific file first — which is exactly the kind of thing that should be a standing instruction rather than something I have to remember to go looking for. The campaign log should stay pure content (what happened, what's open) — the instruction to *maintain* it belongs in the layer that's actually guaranteed to load every time.
